audit_status.source_base: Hebrew Exodus / Shemot covenant ordinances text tradition, draft aligned to public-domain comparison layer later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for restitution, theft, deposits, seduction, vulnerable persons, lending, firstfruits, holiness.

verses.restored: When a man steals ox or sheep and slaughters it or sells it, five cattle he shall repay for the ox, and four flock-animals for the sheep.
If thief is found breaking in and is struck and dies, there is no bloodguilt for him.
If sun has risen upon him, there is bloodguilt for him. Repaying, he shall repay; if he has nothing, he shall be sold for his theft.
If stolen thing is surely found alive in his hand, whether ox, donkey, or sheep, double he shall repay.
When a man causes field or vineyard to be grazed, and sends out his beast and it grazes in another's field, from best of his field and best of his vineyard he shall repay.
When fire goes out and finds thorns, and stacked grain or standing grain or field is consumed, repaying, the one kindling the fire shall repay.
When a man gives to his companion silver or vessels to guard, and it is stolen from house of man, if thief is found, he shall repay double.
If thief is not found, owner of house shall come near to the Divine, to see whether he has stretched his hand against property of his companion.

For every matter of transgression, concerning ox, donkey, sheep, garment, every lost thing of which one says, This is it, the matter of both shall come to the Divine; whom Divine condemns shall repay double to his companion.
When a man gives to his companion donkey, ox, sheep, or any beast to guard, and it dies or is hurt or driven away with no one seeing,
oath of YHWH shall be between them, whether he stretched hand against property of his companion; its owner shall accept, and he shall not repay.
But if stealing, it was stolen from him, he shall repay to its owner.
If tearing, it was torn, he shall bring it as witness; the torn thing he shall not repay.
When a man asks from his companion and it is hurt or dies, its owner not with it, repaying, he shall repay.
If its owner is with it, he shall not repay; if it was hired, it came for its hire.
When a man entices a virgin who is not betrothed and lies with her, bride-price he shall surely give for her to be his wife.
If her father utterly refuses to give her to him, he shall weigh silver according to bride-price of virgins.
You shall not let a sorceress live.
Anyone lying with a beast shall surely be put to death.
One sacrificing to gods, except to YHWH alone, shall be devoted to destruction.
You shall not wrong a sojourner or oppress him, for you were sojourners in land of Egypt.
You shall not afflict any widow or orphan.
If afflicting, you afflict them, and crying, they cry to me, hearing, I will hear their cry.
My anger will burn, and I will kill you with sword, and your women shall become widows and your children orphans.
If you lend silver to my people, to the poor with you, you shall not be to him as creditor; you shall not place interest upon him.
If taking pledge, you take your companion's cloak, before sun goes down you shall return it to him,
for it is his only covering, his garment for his skin; in what shall he lie down? And it shall be, when he cries to me, I will hear, for I am gracious.
You shall not curse Divine, and a leader among your people you shall not curse.
Your fullness and your outflow you shall not delay. Firstborn of your sons you shall give to me.
Thus you shall do with your ox and your flock: seven days it shall be with its mother; on eighth day you shall give it to me.
You shall be holy people to me; flesh torn in field you shall not eat; to dog you shall throw it.

translation_notes: Restitution dominates property injury/theft material: harm requires repair, often more than replacement.
Seduction/bride-price laws are patriarchal and require ethical audit; preserve source but do not present as modern sexual ethics.
Verses 18-20 contain severe holiness prohibitions and require reception-history safeguards, especially around accusations of sorcery.
Sojourner/widow/orphan/poor protections are central: Israel's memory of Egypt becomes anti-oppression law.
No interest on poor and returning cloak before night protect survival dignity.
safety_note: Do not use this chapter to justify violence against accused witches, sexual coercion, patriarchy, or harsh debt practice. The restored layer preserves ancient law; ethical application must be governed by anti-oppression, protection of vulnerable people, and liberation memory.
